description: a gate on the south side of the Angkor Thom temple complex, right next to Baksei Chamkrong, in poor shape, but you can still drive or walk through it, so it makes for some cool photo ops.

Angkor Wat was built between 1113 and 1150 and is the largest religious monument in the world. Half of all tourism to Cambodia is credited to international tourists wanting to visit Angor Wat.

The main attraction being the awe-inspiring ancient temples of Angkor Wat.

get a full day in at Angkor Wat in the morning

small, barely known pyramid temple near Angkor Thom, a hidden gem!

A small, barely known pyramid temple near Angkor Thom. You might have to ask your driver to stop here, because most tours don’t include it in the normal itinerary. This Cambodia temple is a hidden gem!

Banteay Kdei is known as the Citadel of Monks.

more incredible trees and temples, the tree roots at Banteay Kdei are absolutely massive, and they’re taking control of the buildings.
Banteay Samré (named after the ancient people of Indochina)
Banteay Srei (the jewel of Khmer art)

a 1 hour drive from Siem Reap, but it can be combined with a grand/small circuit tour if you pay your driver a bit extra, the red sandstone colors are nice, and it’s known for having some of the best stone carvings out of all the Khmer temples.

Bayon was one of my favorite temples. Its unique construction features a number of smiling stone faces on its towers. There’s a (not-so-secret) spot here where you can get a photo touching noses with one of the statues.

one of the most important temples in Angkor Thom, and it has some of the best wall carvings in the area, famous stone faces that you’ve probably seen in lots of Cambodia photos.

a remote temple you can visit on the way back to Siem Reap, the temple is mostly ruined, but that’s part of what makes it cool!

A temple that is about 1.5 hours drive from Siem Reap, and it can be combined with Koh Ker as a full day trip. The temple is mostly ruined, but that’s part of what makes it cool!

serves fried tarantulas, scorpions, and other bugs to eat, started by a French expat and a Khmer chef, a fusion of local culture as well as the influx of tourism.

A restaurant in Siem Reap where you can eat a tarantula.
Cambodia is one of the few Asian countries that boasts a fairly constant climate year-round, making it a year-round destination.
Visit Cardamom Mountain's jungle or float down on the river on the outskirts of Kampot during these months as the weather is perfect.

East Mebon (used to be on a manmade island)

the temple is not in great shape, but the highlight here would have to be the elephant statues in each corner of the temple.

This temple is not in great shape compared to some of the others in Cambodia, but the highlight here would have to be the elephant statues in each corner of the temple.

The owner of Les Manguiers suggested I take a boat up the river and get dropped off with a kayak to paddle my way down through the Emerald Palace loop and to make a stop at a café on the riverside. The entire kayak trip felt like I was in the Jungle Book as I paddled through the lush tropical loop.

The Epic Arts Center was developed for local disabled children as a haven to establish their own community and receive artistic education. The owner's daughter Marie led us to the center and we had a full day of arts, crafts and got to enjoy a performance by the kids.

The Epic Arts Center was just a short bike ride away so the owner’s young daughter, Marie, led me and an Australian family staying at the guesthouse into town. The Epic Arts Cambodia Center was developed for local disabled children as a safe haven to establish their own community and receive artistic education. Once we arrived at the center, we participated in several workshops set up by the children, painted, learned some sign language, and even some dance routines. The local children who participate in the Epic Arts programs are taught gardening, crafting, dancing, painting, and music. Arts and crafts taught by the children After all of the workshops finished up, a snack of local fruits and nuts was served, and then the children got ready to put on a performance. A group of deaf children put on multiple modern and interpretive dance performances, while another group of deaf boys danced, juggled, and performed a comedic skit with stunts, and another group of blind children played their instruments beautifully. Snack time! I was completely awestruck during the whole performance. I was laughing and almost crying by the end of it. The children were so talented and so happy. It was very apparent that Epic Arts had created a place where the children could feel a part of something very special, with other children they could relate to, without the burdens of a society that would normally make them feel outcast.

The resort features solar-powered accommodation, healthy, home-grown foods, the sound of the Cambodian jungle, magnificent views across islands to Vietnam, and the sweet fragrance of jasmine and jungle blossom. The owner, Owen, and his beautiful wife, Jasmine, keep the resort running using as few resources as possible. It’s the perfect “off-the-grid” accommodation for travelers to relax, completely.

A small French-Cambodian town in the south of Cambodia.

Visit Cardamom Mountain's jungle or float down on the river on the outskirts of Kampot during these months as the weather is perfect.

The area is just outside of Kampot and sits on the Kampot River. It is absolutely beautiful! Driving through the Rice Fields, I decided to spend my morning kayaking. The owner of Les Manguiers suggested I take a boat up the river and get dropped off with a kayak to paddle my way down through the Emerald Palace loop and to make a stop at a café on the riverside.

The area is just outside of Kampot and sits on the Kampot River. It is absolutely beautiful! Driving through the Rice Fields, the river houses of Kampot, and stopping to soak up some sun.

Kep was once Cambodia’s most popular and prestigious beach destination for the French and Cambodian elite. But, after decades of civil war, the town is now haunted by the abandoned remains of old French villas. While much of Kep was destroyed during the Khmer Rouge days, some of the charm still remains. The national park, an island archipelago, beautiful beaches, fresh crab, and world-famous black pepper are just some of the reasons to visit Kep.

Kep is only 30 minutes from the Vietnamese border so I'll hopefully be moving on to Vietnam the day after next. I had such a wonderful time in Kampot and can't wait to see Kep tomorrow!

Kep is only 30 minutes from the Vietnamese border so I'll hopefully be moving on to Vietnam the day after next. Louise gave me some advice on traveling in Vietnam and where to stay tomorrow night in Kep.

The market is famous for its fresh crab and is a must-visit when in Kep. The view from the market is absolutely surreal, with the sea air blowing through your hair and the sound of waves crashing onto the shore.
Khmer Grill is a restaurant that serves normal cuisine, with options like $1 beef kabobs. The restaurant is a great place to get a bona fide meal after trying the bug-based dishes at The Bugs Cafe.
a group of remote temples about 2.5 hours drive from Siem Reap, the main building is a 7-tier pyramid in the forest, and a stairway leads to the top where you can get a nice view of the whole area.

We made reservations at Koulen Restaurant for dinner and a traditional Apsara dance performance. The dinner was set up buffet style and literally had every Cambodian dish you could think of… and then some!

a 4-star hotel in Siem Reap, $18 USD per night for a clean double room with cold A/C, work desk, refrigerator, wardrobe, and more, there's also an on-site restaurant and pool, the staff there is amazingly friendly, and I love the epic wall art showing scenes from the Angkor Wat.

La Residence Blanc D'Angkor is a 4-star hotel that offers clean double rooms with cold A/C, work desk, refrigerator, wardrobe, and more. The hotel has an on-site restaurant and pool, and the staff is amazingly friendly. The hotel is a great place to stay while exploring Siem Reap and the surrounding area.

We stayed at the 4-star La Residence Blanc D'Angkor in Siem Reap. It was $18 USD per night for a clean double room with cold A/C, work desk, refrigerator, wardrobe, and more. There's also an on-site restaurant and pool. The staff there is amazingly friendly, and I love the epic wall art showing scenes from the Angkor Wat. It sets the mood for exploring some Cambodian temple ruins.

Les Manguiers, or 'Mango' in English, is a little French getaway in Kampot, Cambodia that was recommended to me by a fellow hostel mate in Bangkok. She said it was a MUST for anyone traveling through Cambodia in need of a little R&R. The owners are French Cambodian so it has a lot of beautiful European influence. I got my things settled in my room and enjoyed dinner in a bungalow on the river. The sunset was incredible and just as usual during my time in Cambodia, a lightening storm lit up the sky for the rest of the evening.

The owner at my bed & breakfast, Les Manguiers, mentioned that there would be a performance at the local center for disabled children.
The waterfalls of Rattanakiri and Mondulkiri are overflowing, making them great swimming spots.
an island temple in the middle of a little pond, looks better right after the rainy season, late in the dry season there’s not much water left.
Neak Poan (the sacred island)

There are two reflective ponds in front of the Angkor Wat temples that serve as the main photo spots for the sunrise. You can see them in the Google map above. There are some slight differences in the view you get at each pond, but these are pretty subtle. The last time I went to Angkor Wat was in May, and it was very much the dry season, so the north pond had no water in it and that means no reflection for photos.
Onederz Hostel Siem Reap is a great option and offers daily tours and recommendations — it has private and dorm room options.
Phnom Bakheng is a symbolic representation of Mount Meru, home of the Hindu gods. We ended our day here and made the long climb to the top to watch the sunset. It was breathtaking!

My friend Lindsay and I had returned to Phnom Penh after visiting the Temples of Angkor and we had to organize a Tuk Tuk to take us to our separate destinations… Lindsay to the airport and Kampot for me.

After landing, we had to get our Cambodian visas.

In Phnom Penh in November, the Water Festival aka Bon Om Touk is held.

The Royal Palace Phnom Penh is a big area near the riverfront with more than a dozen fancy palaces, shrines, and stupas to check out. The Throne Hall is the main building of the Royal Palace, built in 1863, but it's still used sometimes for religious and royal ceremonies. Sadly you can't go inside, or take photos inside. But you're allowed to peek in the windows, and the staff didn't really seem to monitor people taking photos through the windows. Still, the best photo ops are when you walk around the outside of the building and take in the stunning architecture of the rooftops. It's quite a view. The best time to photograph it is in the morning.

place: Phnom Penh Royal Palace & Silver Pagoda
description: Phnom Penh doesn’t have a whole lot of things to see and do compared to other capital cities in SE Asia like Bangkok or even Vientiane, but Phnom Penh Royal Palace & Silver Pagoda is one of the places to visit
The Phochani Pavilion is a dance hall located within the Royal Palace grounds.

an incredible spot that’s part of the Koh Ker area, these are ancient temples being swallowed up by huge strangler trees and you must see them while you’re nearby.

Ancient temples being swallowed up by huge strangler trees and you must see them if you have time to take a day trip out of Siem Reap. This may be my favorite Cambodia temple after the Angkor Wat itself.

Pre Rup (a funeral temple)

a big temple near the end of the grand circuit, funerals were done here, so that may be why the temple looks so creepy, the buildings at the top of the pyramid are in the best shape, so be sure to take a look up there.

Preah Khan (contains a stupa that looks like a lit candle from a sunlit opening)

most of the main temple is ruined, but it’s cool to see how nature is reclaiming what’s left of it.

Most of the main temple is ruined, but it’s cool to see how nature is reclaiming what’s left of it. This is one of my favorite stops on the grand circuit.
The waterfalls of Rattanakiri and Mondulkiri are overflowing, making them great swimming spots.

a group of temples just 30 minutes east of Siem Reap, these date back to the 9th century, making them some of the oldest Khmer temples in Cambodia.

A group of temples that date back to the 9th century, making them some of the oldest Khmer temples in Cambodia. They are only a 30 minute drive from Siem Reap, making it easy to combine with something else as a full day trip.

We decided to catch a night bus to Siem Reap so as not to waste any time and get a full day in at Angkor Wat in the morning.

Siem Reap, Cambodia

The Siem Reap area of Cambodia is home to the world famous Angkor Wat ruins, which bring in millions of tourists every year. As a gateway to the famous Angkor Wat temples, Siem Reap is the cornerstone of Cambodia’s tourism and it’s definitely one of the best travel destinations

the riverside markets in Siem Reap are great for souvenir shopping, street food, and street photography, the stalls have all kinds of items for sale, from paintings and clothing, to fridge magnets and postcards.

A market in Siem Reap where you can shop for souvenirs.

The night markets were better than any market I went to in Thailand – less expensive, and less touristy.

You definitely don't want to miss Siem Reap's Night Market!
Humidity can reach up to 80% every day and heavy downpours dampen trips to places like Cardamom and Sihanoukville.

The Silver Pagoda, also known as Wat Preah Keo, was first built in 1892, but then it was expensively rebuilt in 1962 with a massive amount of gold, silver, and diamonds. The name of the pagoda comes from the silver floor inside, which has almost 5,000 silver tiles weighing over 5 tons. Photography is not allowed inside the building, but the outside is really amazing and it's great for photos at any time of the day.

I like the south pond better, because a palm tree hides the fact that there’s renovation work being done on one of the towers. However, many people also seem to prefer the north pond. Which pond you use, though, will ultimately depend on the season and how much rain there’s been. The last time I went to Angkor Wat was in May, and it was very much the dry season, so the north pond had no water in it and that means no reflection for photos. The good news is that the ponds are only about 50 meters apart. The south pond had plenty of water even in the dry season, so naturally that’s where I did my photos.

The restaurant is located on the beach and serves fresh seafood, including crab. The view from the table is absolutely surreal, with the sun burning the sky in orange and pink as rays of light reflect off the surface of the ocean and glimmer endlessly towards the horizon.

The Angkor temple Ta Prohm is easily recognized (and famed) as the set for the movie Tomb Raider. Large vines cover the ruins creating a fantasy setting like something from a storybook.

To avoid crowds, visit smaller stops like Ta Prohm and Kampot.

the famous ‘Tomb Raider temple’ used in the 2001 Angelina Jolie movie, an incredible tree here that is growing on top of one of the temples, a must see.

Ta Som (overgrown by a sacred fig tree)

a smaller temple, but the best part is the stone doorway being consumed and held in place by a strangler tree.

My friend Lindsay and I had returned to Phnom Penh after visiting the Temples of Angkor and we had to organize a Tuk Tuk to take us to our separate destinations… Lindsay to the airport and Kampot for me.

My friend Lindsay and I had returned to Phnom Penh after visiting the Temples of Angkor

The Bugs Cafe is an insect restaurant that specializes in bug dining, with a menu that includes tarantulas, ants, scorpions, grasshoppers, and bug larvae. The restaurant is well managed, and the chefs know what they're doing. The style and preparation of everything is top notch. The waiters are funny and friendly, and can explain anything on the menu. The restaurant is located in the city center of Siem Reap, and can be reached on foot or by taxi.

Are you looking for one of the craziest and most unique dining experiences in Southeast Asia? This is the Bugs Cafe, an insect restaurant in Siem Reap
one of the less known temples in Angkor Thom, and most tour groups don’t seem to go here, the carvings and doorways are good to explore.
Tonle Sap Lake swells up to five times its normal size and is home to many floating villages and flooded forests.

a Cambodian temple that’s also notable for being used as a killing field during the Khmer Rouge communist rule in Cambodia during the 1970s, there is a stupa memorial filled with skulls and bones from the victims of the genocide.

A killing fields site in Siem Reap.
